elements.sent-2411-001: If $f: \mathbb{N \rightarrow \{\pm1\}$ is a sample of the random completely multiplicative function, we show that almost surely $\sum_{n \le x \frac{f(n) {\sqrt{n }$ changes signs infinitely many times, answering a question of Aymone.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Let $f: \mathbb{N \rightarrow \{\pm1\}$ be the random completely multiplicative function, defined by $f(p) = \pm 1$ with probability $\frac{1 {2}$ independently at the primes, and then extended multiplicatively to the natural numbers.}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Given $\alpha \in [0,1]$, Aymone \cite{aymone considered the question of sign changes in partial sums of \begin{equation}\sum_{n} \frac{f(n)}{n^{\alpha}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-004: For $\alpha > \frac{1 {2}$ the sum is known to converge to a positive number with probability $1$ as $x \rightarrow \infty$ (namely, it converges to its Euler product), therefore changes signs finitely many times.}
elements.sent-2411-005: For $\alpha < \frac{1 {2}$ he stablished that the sum changes sign infinitely many times as $x \rightarrow \infty$ with probability $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper we settle the case $\alpha = \frac{1 {2}$ that was previously left open:.}
elements.sent-2411-007: If $f$ is a sample of the random completely multiplicative function then with probability $1$ the sum \begin{equation}S_x = \sum_{n \le x} \frac{f(n)}{\sqrt{n}}\end{equation} changes signs infinitely many times as $x \rightarrow \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our method is akin to \cite{Baker1990 , using oscilations in $\sum \frac{f(n) {n^s}$ for real $s \rightarrow \frac{1 {2 ^+$ to produce sign changes, without using ever using complex values of $s$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: By integration by parts for $t>0$ \begin{equation}F(t) = \int_{1}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+t}} = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{f(n)}{n^{\frac{1}{2}+t}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-010: Where we use the fact that $\sum_{n=1 ^{\infty \frac{f(n) {n^{\frac{1 {2 +t }$ converges almost surely for $t>0$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: If $S_x$ is positive for $x \ge X_0$, then \begin{equation}F(t) = \int_{1}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+t}} \ge \int_{X_0}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+t}} - C' \ge \int_{X_0}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+2t}} - C' \ge \int_{1}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+2t}} - C = F(2t)-C,\end{equation} so in that case one has $F(t) \ge F(2t)-C$ for every $t>0$ where $C$ is a constant depending only on $X_0$ (if $S_n$ is eventually negative we can obtain $F(t) \le F(2t)+C$ and make a similar argument then).
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}F(t) = \int_{1}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+t}} \ge \int_{X_0}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+t}} - C' \ge \int_{X_0}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+2t}} - C' \ge \int_{1}^{\infty} S_x \frac{dx}{x^{1+2t}} - C = F(2t)-C,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-012: From now on, assume $t$ belongs to a sequence $t_1,t_2,...$ tending to zero quickly, say $t_i = 2^{-2^{i }$.}
elements.sent-2411-013: By the almost sure Euler product we have \begin{equation}F(t) = \frac{1}{t} \prod_p \left(1-\frac{f(p)}{p^{\frac{1}{2}+t}}\right)^{-1} = \exp\left(\sum_p \frac{f(p)}{p^{\frac{1}{2}+t}}+ \frac{3}{2} \log \frac{1}{t} + O(1)\right).\end{equation}.
